channel.title: The Chat on Lumen AI — User Guide
channel.description: Recent content in The Chat on Lumen AI — User Guide

item.title: Typing & Sending
item.description:
The bottom of the screen is where you type. It looks and works like any messaging app — but it has a few extra buttons that let you control where Lumen looks for answers.

The Input Bar

At the bottom of every chat is the input bar. Here’s what each element does:

Clinical Database
My Docs
Web
Message Lumen…
↑

Knowledge Source Toggles

Below the text field are three toggles that control where Lumen searches for information:

item.title: Reading Answers
item.description:
Lumen’s answers look like regular text, but they often include extra pieces — like a list of sources, a progress indicator showing what it searched, or a tappable card with a detailed report. Here’s what each part means.

Anatomy of a Response

Every assistant response follows a consistent structure. Here’s what you’ll see, from top to bottom:

✦
Chat
What is the CHA₂DS₂-VASc score for a 72-year-old woman with hypertension and diabetes?
✦
Thought for 3s
Ran medical calculator
CHA₂DS₂-VASc · age=72, sex=female

The CHA₂DS₂-VASc score is 5 for this patient, which indicates a high stroke risk. Anticoagulation is recommended.

item.title: How Lumen Thinks
item.description:
You wouldn’t trust a colleague who just said “trust me” without explaining their reasoning. Neither should you trust an AI that way. That’s why Lumen shows you how it arrived at its answer — you can expand the thinking block and see the logic step by step.

What Is the Thinking Block?

When Lumen processes a complex question, it first “thinks” through the problem before responding. This appears as a collapsible block at the top of the response.

item.title: Reports & Files
item.description:
Sometimes a text answer isn’t enough. When you ask about drug interactions, Lumen doesn’t just tell you — it creates a detailed report you can tap to open. Ask for a presentation, and it builds one you can download. These attached files and reports are called artifacts, and they show up as tappable cards in the chat.

What Are Artifacts?

An artifact is a structured, interactive piece of content that Lumen creates during a conversation. They appear as clickable cards within the chat.

item.title: Where It Gets Its Info
item.description:
Lumen always shows its work. After every answer, you’ll see a “Sources” link that tells you exactly which guidelines, studies, or databases the answer came from — and how closely each one matched your question. Think of it as the bibliography at the end of a paper.

The Sources Chip

After a response that draws on evidence, you’ll see a sources chip:

📚 Sources (4)

Click this chip to expand the list of sources inline, or click “View in panel” to open them in the side panel for a detailed view.

item.title: Uploading Your Files
item.description:
Have a research paper you want summarized? A lab report you want interpreted? A protocol you want compared to current guidelines? Just attach the file to your chat and ask away. Lumen reads the document and uses it to answer your question.

How to Attach Files

1

Click the paperclip icon

In the input bar, click the paperclip icon on the left side. This opens the document tray.
2

Choose or upload a file

You can either:
